paraphrase: According to Ser Barristan Selmy, who was a Kingsguard at the time, King Aerys 'wanted' Joanna Lannister for years.
At Joanna and Tywin's wedding, a drunken Aerys made a joke implying he wanted to sleep with Joanna, and later that night he took 'liberties' with her.
Whatever Aerys did to Joanna that night probably wasn't sex, and it was too early for it to have been the actual conception of Tyrion anyway.
Even so, the wedding-night incident shows King Aerys lusted after Tyrion's mother and acted on it, and as king he probably could have had her whenever he wanted.
In A Storm of Swords, Joffrey tells Sansa that kings often sleep with whoever they want, including married women, supporting the idea Aerys could have had Joanna despite her marriage to Tywin.
It has been questioned whether Joanna was even at King's Landing with Aerys at the time Tyrion was conceived.
Tywin was presumably in King's Landing as Hand of the King around that time, and Joanna must have been with Tywin, not Aerys, if that's when Tyrion was conceived.

Joanna was apparently at Casterly Rock 'shortly' before giving birth to Tyrion (she died in childbirth), so it's unclear whether conception happened in King's Landing with Aerys or at Casterly Rock — overall the timeline is inconclusive.

It's conceivable Tywin never knew Tyrion wasn't his biological son — Joanna could have been threatened into secrecy — and Tywin's total obliviousness to Jaime and Cersei's incest shows he can be impressively ignorant of his loved ones' sexual secrets.
It makes more sense that Tywin did know or suspect Tyrion wasn't his son, partly because Tywin 'outright says it a couple of times.'
The usual explanation for Tywin's hatred of Tyrion — that Tyrion killed Joanna in childbirth — is called out by the video as 'a ridiculous reason to hate the kid,' implying a deeper cause is needed.

spoken_quote: all the drains and cisterns within Casterly Rock
paraphrase: The other common explanation — that Tywin is disgusted by Tyrion's public drinking and whoring — is also weak, since Tywin was already cruel to Tyrion well before that behavior started: at sixteen Tyrion was refused travel and put in charge of 'all the drains and cisterns within Casterly Rock,' and at thirteen his beloved Tysha was raped on Tywin's orders.
verbatim: Lord Tywin had put an end to that hope ten days before his dwarf son’s sixteenth nameday, when Tyrion asked to tour the Nine Free Cities, as his uncles had done at that same age. “My brothers could be relied upon to bring no shame upon House Lannister,” his father had replied. “Neither ever wed a whore.” And when Tyrion had reminded him that in ten days he would be a man grown, free to travel where he wished, Lord Tywin had said, “No man is free. Only children and fools think elsewise. Go, by all means. Wear motley and stand upon your head to amuse the spice lords and the cheese kings. Just see that you pay your own way and put aside any thoughts of returning.” At that the boy’s defiance had crumbled. “If it is useful occupation you require, useful occupation you shall have,” his father then said. So to mark his manhood, Tyrion was given charge of all the drains and cisterns within Casterly Rock. Perhaps he hoped I’d fall into one. But Tywin had been disappointed in that. The drains never drained half so well as when he had charge of them.

paraphrase: By contrast, Jaime and Cersei have their own personality defects but Tywin doesn't mistreat them without good reason, suggesting Tyrion's especially harsh treatment requires a special explanation.
Tywin disowns Tyrion despite being obsessed with his own legacy — seemingly self-defeating unless Tywin doesn't actually consider Tyrion his true heir.

When Aerys made Jaime a Kingsguard, making him ineligible to inherit Casterly Rock, that would have left Aerys's own bastard Tyrion as heir to Tywin's legacy — spitting in the face of everything Tywin worked for, which explains Tywin disowning Tyrion, treating him harshly, and sacking Aerys's city (King's Landing) shortly after.
Tywin still lets Tyrion live as a Lannister at all, likely because Tyrion is still Joanna's son — the woman Tywin loved more than anything — something like a deathbed-promise situation.

Tywin funds Tyrion generously and lets him live comfortably as a member of a great house, but also seems to periodically try to get him killed — such as placing him on the front lines at the Battle of the Blackwater for no clear reason, perhaps to remove him without directly kinslaying.
It's a common Targaryen trait to have dreams of dragons, often prophetic ones — Daenerys, Aemon, Daeron, and Daemon Targaryen are all known to have had dragon dreams.
Tyrion is also linked to dragons, but there's no evidence of him having actual literal prophetic dragon dreams like the other Targaryens — the likely explanation is that he simply likes dragons.

Tyrion's pale-blond hair (paired with black) more closely resembles Targaryen silver-blond than the golden-blond hair typical of Lannisters, and per the 'seed is strong' principle established early in the series, hair colour can indicate parentage.

Tyrion has one green eye and one black eye; the only other character in the series noted with mismatched eyes is Shiera Seastar, a bastard daughter of Aegon IV Targaryen, symbolically linking two-coloured eyes to mixed Targaryen blood.

It's been suggested Tyrion's dwarfism could be the result of Targaryen genetic weirdness or an attempted abortion, but there's no precedent in the story for either occurring, so this isn't strong evidence.

If Tyrion is a Targaryen he could fit the 'three heads of the dragon' prophecy alongside Dany and Jon — all three would have Targaryen blood, each had a mother who died in childbirth, each has had to kill someone they love, and each could correspond to one of the three living dragons.

The video questions whether prophecy actually functions reliably in the story at all, noting we're several thousand pages in and have never clearly seen prophecy 'at work,' making the three-heads-of-the-dragon prophecy a shaky reason to believe Tyrion is a Targaryen.

Genna Lannister says that, more than Jaime, Tyrion is Tywin's true son — sharing his cunning, calculating nature, and political skill, just delivered in the body of a dwarf that Tywin cannot bring himself to respect.

Tyrion's drinking and whoring are a consequence of his father's abuse, and ironically Tywin hates Tyrion for vices Tywin shares himself — Tyrion found Shae in Tywin's own bed, mirroring how Tywin's father Tytos let a common mistress take his dead wife's place and wear her jewellery late in his life.

After Tytos's death, young Tywin punished that mistress cruelly, running her naked through the streets of Lannisport and forcing her to publicly confess to being 'a thief and a harlot' — yet late in his own life Tywin repeats the same pattern with Shae, letting her wear his Hand of the King jewellery.
verbatim: My lord father had no use for whores, she thought. After our mother died he never touched a woman. She gave the guardsman a chilly look. “This is not . . . when Lord Tywin’s father died he returned to Casterly Rock to find a . . . a woman of this sort . . . bedecked in his lady mother’s jewels, wearing one of her gowns. He stripped them off her, and all else as well. For a fortnight she was paraded naked through the streets of Lannisport, to confess to every man she met that she was a thief and a harlot. That was how Lord Tywin Lannister dealt with whores. He never . . . this woman was here for some other purpose, not for . . .”

spoken_quote: you are ... no son of mine
paraphrase: When the dying Tywin tells Tyrion 'you are ... no son of mine,' the video argues this doesn't mean Tyrion is a secret Targaryen bastard — it means Tywin rejected his trueborn son because he couldn't bear to look upon a twisted version of himself, and Tyrion then kills Tywin with the same cold brutality his father embodies.
